# Recent progress of photothermal catalysts for carbon dioxide conversion

![](data:image/png;base64,)
![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Sang Hun Choi1

### Inhak Song1,2,\*

### Wan Jae Dong1,2,\*

## Abstract

Photothermal catalysis has emerged as a promising strategy for converting carbon dioxide (CO2) into value-added chemicals and fuels, offering a dual-energy approach that combines light and thermal energy to drive reactions under mild conditions. Photothermal effects are usually demonstrated by using plasmonic nanoparticles, which generate hot carriers and localized heating through light absorption. These effects facilitate chemical reactions by lowering activation barriers and increasing reaction rates. The synergy between hot carrier-induced redox reactions and thermocatalytic processes driven by localized heating allows for the activation of challenging reactions with reduced energy inputs. The balance between these pathways can be optimized through rational design of photothermal catalysts. In this review, we highlight recent advancements in catalyst materials, especially emphasizing the importance of photothermal effects to achieve higher efficiencies in CO2 conversion reactions such as CO2 hydrogenation and dry reforming of methane, both of which are vital for reducing greenhouse gases and producing clean fuels. Finally, the current challenges, outlook, and new strategies for catalyst optimization will be discussed to realize the full potential of photothermal catalysis in creating a sustainable and low-carbon energy future.

## Graphical Abstract

![](https://image.oaes.cc/ff07bb3d-2b6c-47ac-aca7-74a0eeeada2b/em40227-coverimg.big.jpg)

## Keywords

## INTRODUCTION

Carbon dioxide (CO2) conversion into value-added chemicals and fuels is an essential strategy to transition towards a sustainable energy future[[1](#B1)-[3](#B3)]. The increasing levels of atmospheric CO2 concentration require innovative approaches for carbon capture and utilization. Among the various techniques developed for CO2 conversion, photothermal catalysis has emerged as a particularly promising method[[4](#B4)-[8](#B8)]. By harnessing both light and thermal energy, photothermal catalysts can drive chemical reactions under mild conditions, offering a synergistic effect that reduces operating temperature and enhances efficiency. This dual-energy approach enables reactions that are often more challenging or less efficient when using conventional thermal or photocatalytic methods independently.

One of the most studied reactions in photothermal CO2 conversion is CO2 hydrogenation, a process that reduces CO2 to produce valuable hydrocarbons and alcohols, such as carbon monoxide (CO), methane (CH4), and methanol[[9](#B9)-[17](#B17)]. Photothermal CO2 hydrogenation benefits from the combined effects of light absorption and localized heating, which facilitates the activation of CO2 molecules and their subsequent hydrogenation reaction with gaseous hydrogen (H2). This reaction not only holds promise for producing clean fuels but also offers a pathway for integrating renewable solar energy into chemical processes. Recent advancements in catalyst design have significantly improved the efficiency and selectivity of photothermal CO2 hydrogenation.

Another important photothermal CO2 conversion catalysis is the dry reforming of methane (DRM). This reaction simultaneously converts two major greenhouse gases (CO2 and CH4) into syngas (a mixture of H2 and CO), which serve as a precursor for various industrial chemicals and fuels[[18](#B18)-[24](#B24)]. In conventional thermocatalytic DRM, the reaction typically requires high temperatures of 700-900 °C, which are necessary to overcome the thermodynamic barriers. However, these harsh conditions pose several challenges, including the high energy input and the carbon coking on the catalyst surface, which leads to rapid catalyst deactivation. Photothermal catalysis offers a promising solution to these challenges by enabling DRM to proceed at significantly lower temperatures while maintaining high conversion efficiencies. Recent studies have shown that photothermal catalysts can mitigate carbon coking due to the photo-excited charge carriers and enhanced reaction kinetics.

This review will provide an in-depth examination of recent progress in photothermal catalysis, with a focus on two key CO2 conversion reactions: CO2 hydrogenation and DRM. We will introduce the underlying mechanisms that drive the enhanced catalytic activity observed in photothermal processes. This review also explores the latest advancements in catalyst materials, including the design of nanostructured and hybrid systems that have demonstrated superior performance. Finally, we will address the current challenges and perspectives, such as the need for a deeper understanding of the photothermal effects and the development of durable catalysts. Through this comprehensive review, we aim to provide future directions and opportunities for research in photothermal catalysis, emphasizing its potential to contribute to a low-carbon and sustainable energy future.

## FUNDAMENTALS OF PHOTOTHERMAL CATALYSIS

### Photothermal effects

Photothermal catalysis is an emerging field that combines light and thermal energy to drive chemical reactions with improved efficiency and selectivity. A key aspect of this approach is the photothermal effects, which involve converting absorbed photons into heat while simultaneously generating high-energy charge carriers, often called hot carriers. These photothermal effects arise from several interconnected processes, including plasmonic light absorption, hot carrier generation, photon-to-phonon conversion, local heating, and heat transfer/dissipation [[Figure 1](#fig1)]. Understanding these mechanisms is significant for designing effective photothermal catalysts for CO2 conversion reactions.

![](https://image.oaes.cc/ff07bb3d-2b6c-47ac-aca7-74a0eeeada2b/em40227.fig.1.jpg)

Figure 1. Key processes in the photothermal effect. (A) Light absorption at specific resonant wavelengths occurs through plasmonic excitation within metal nanoparticles, generating high-energy charge carriers. (B) Hot electrons (e-) can directly participate in CO2 reduction reactions when the metal nanoparticle forms a junction with a p-type semiconductor, while in n-type semiconductors, hot holes (h+) can facilitate oxidation reactions. (C) Photon-to-phonon conversion within the crystal lattice of metal nanoparticles leads to localized heating. (D) The accumulated heat is subsequently transferred or dissipated to the surrounding environment or support material, contributing to a temperature gradient.

#### Plasmonic light absorption

Plasmonic light absorption is the first and the most important step for photothermal catalysis, primarily occurring in metal nanoparticles (NPs) with free electrons [[Figure 1A](#fig1)]. These NPs possess unique optical properties due to the collective oscillation of free electrons when exposed to light at specific wavelengths[[25](#B25)]. This resonance results in the strong absorption of light and the concentration of electromagnetic fields at the surface. The plasmonic resonance wavelength can be finely tuned by modifying the size, shape, and composition of the NPs, allowing for precise control over the light absorption spectrum due to factors such as surface scattering and radiation damping[[26](#B26)-[28](#B28)]. For instance, when the mean free path of conduction electrons becomes comparable to the particle size, there is increased electron-surface collisions and a blue shift in the surface plasmon resonance peak[[29](#B29)]. In contrast, when NPs get larger, they exhibit redshifts due to enhanced radiative losses and dynamic depolarization effects. The shape of NPs also plays a crucial role in determining plasmonic properties. For example, nanorods exhibit two surface plasmon resonance modes: a transverse mode and a longitudinal mode that shifts to longer wavelengths as the aspect ratio increases. Upon light absorption, plasmonic NPs transfer energy to free electrons, generating a non-equilibrium distribution of energetic electrons and holes, known as hot carriers.

#### Hot carrier generation and redox reactions

The absorption of light by plasmonic metal NPs leads to the generation of hot carriers (electrons and holes) which have energies significantly higher than the thermal equilibrium state. This process begins with the excitation of electrons to higher energy states within the conduction band, leaving behind corresponding holes in the valence band [[Figure 1B](#fig1)]. Hot electrons generated through plasmonic excitation have energies that exceed the Fermi level of the metal, enabling them to overcome activation barriers for specific reactions[[30](#B30),[31](#B31)]. For instance, in CO2 reduction reactions, hot electrons can reduce CO2 molecules adsorbed on the surface, while the corresponding hot holes can oxidize other reactants, completing the catalytic cycle. The direct involvement of hot carriers in redox reactions resembles conventional photocatalysis, where photo-excited electrons and holes in the semiconductors participate in redox reactions[[32](#B32),[33](#B33)]. The lifetime and energy distribution of hot carriers are influenced by various factors, such as the size and shape of the plasmonic NPs and the properties of the surrounding medium. Hybrid materials that combine plasmonic metals with semiconductors are particularly effective because they facilitate charge separation, prolonging the lifetime of hot carriers and enhancing the ability to participate in catalytic reactions. In these materials, hot electrons can be injected into the conduction band of the semiconductor, while hot holes remain in the metal, enabling spatially separated redox processes[[34](#B34),[35](#B35)].

#### Photon-to-phonon conversion and local heating

Not all the energy absorbed by plasmonic NPs is converted into hot carriers; most of the absorbed photon energy is dissipated as heat through photon-to-phonon conversion[[36](#B36)]. When hot carriers relax back to their equilibrium state, they release energy in the form of phonons, which are quantized vibrations of the crystal lattice [[Figure 1C](#fig1)]. This energy release results in localized heating of the NP and its surroundings, leading to a rapid increase in temperature at the nanoscale[[37](#B37),[38](#B38)]. Local heating is a key feature of photothermal catalysis and plays a vital role in enhancing reaction kinetics. Localized heating can enable the reaction to proceed under milder conditions, reducing the overall energy input. The extent of local heating depends on several factors, including the amount of absorbed photons, the thermal conductivity of the metal NPs and support materials, and the efficiency of heat dissipation to the surrounding environment[[39](#B39),[40](#B40)].

#### Heat transfer

Heat transfer is a critical aspect of photothermal catalysis, as it determines how the heat generated by photon-to-phonon conversion is distributed across the catalyst and the surrounding medium [[Figure 1D](#fig1)]. For chemical reactions that require high temperatures, it is crucial to confine heat locally to maintain elevated temperatures. In contrast, efficient heat transfer is essential to maintain the desired reaction environment and prevent overheating of the catalyst working at low temperatures. In photothermal systems, heat can be transferred through conduction, convection, and radiation, with conduction being the primary mode at the nanoscale[[41](#B41),[42](#B42)]. Design of photothermal catalysts must consider the thermal properties of both the active material and the support. Moreover, spatial distribution of heat within the catalyst can influence the selectivity of the reaction. In some cases, temperature gradients created by localized heating can drive specific reaction pathways, favoring the formation of desired products. To optimize heat transfer in photothermal catalysts, researchers have explored core-shell structures, where a plasmonic core is encapsulated by a shell material with tailored thermal properties[[9](#B9),[40](#B40),[43](#B43),[44](#B44)]. For example, applying nanoporous oxide materials on top of the metal NPs can create a nanoscale greenhouse effect, achieving high local temperatures[[9](#B9)]. This design enables precise control over the thermal environment and can enhance the durability of the catalyst under operating conditions.

### Interplay between photothermal effects and catalytic reactions

The integration of photothermal effects into catalytic reactions represents a promising strategy for enhancing reaction efficiency, productivity, and stability. When plasmonic NPs absorb light, they primarily generate two types of energy: hot carriers and thermal energy. These energies drive chemical reactions via two major pathways: hot carrier-induced redox reactions and thermocatalytic reactions facilitated by localized heating [[Figure 2](#fig2)]. Understanding the interplay between these pathways is crucial for optimizing photothermal catalysis.

![](https://image.oaes.cc/ff07bb3d-2b6c-47ac-aca7-74a0eeeada2b/em40227.fig.2.jpg)

Figure 2. (A) Plasmonic photocatalysis driven by photo-excited hot charge carriers. (B) Plasmonic thermocatalysis driven by heat energy generated from solar light. (C) Reduction of activation energy barrier by local hot charge carriers (photocatalysis) and acceleration of thermocatalytic reaction kinetics due to Boltzmann thermal energy (KBT) supplied to the reactants surrounding the plasmonic nanoparticle.

#### Hot carrier-induced redox reactions

When plasmonic NPs absorb light, the energy is transferred to free electrons, generating a non-equilibrium distribution of energetic electrons and holes, commonly referred to as hot carriers. These hot carriers can participate in driving redox reactions at the NP surface similar to conventional photocatalysis [[Figure 2A](#fig2)]. Due to their high energy, hot carriers can overcome the activation energy barrier for electron transfer processes, facilitating reactions that would otherwise be thermodynamically unfavorable. The efficiency of this pathway depends heavily on the lifetime and energy of the hot carriers.

#### Thermocatalytic reactions driven by local heating

The photothermal effects generate localized heating through photon-to-phonon conversion. This thermal energy can drive thermocatalytic reactions, where the increase in temperature enhances the reaction rate by providing the necessary activation energy [[Figure 2B](#fig2)][[42](#B42)]. The elevated local temperature can enhance the rate of bond cleavage and formation, leading to higher catalytic activity. The thermal effect also influences the kinetics of the reaction, often making the reaction pathway similar to traditional thermocatalysis. However, the localized and transient nature of the heating can lead to unique reaction dynamics that are distinct from conventional thermal catalysis.

#### Synergistic effects between hot carriers and thermal energy

The most compelling aspect of photothermal catalysis is the synergy between hot carriers and thermal energy, which together provide a dual enhancement to the catalytic process. When both pathways are engaged, the reaction can overcome the activation energy barrier through two complementary mechanisms [[Figure 2C](#fig2)]. The first mechanism is related to the thermal effect, which increases the thermal energy of the system, often expressed as KbT, where Kb is the Boltzmann constant and T is the absolute temperature. This thermal energy corresponds to the average kinetic energy of the reactant molecules. As the temperature rises, the kinetic energy of the molecules increases, leading to more frequent and energetic collisions between them. These higher-energy collisions provide sufficient energy to surpass the activation energy barrier. Secondly, the hot carriers can modify the electronic structure of the surface and induce electronic interactions that further reduce the activation barrier as photocatalytic reactions. This combined effect allows for the activation of otherwise challenging reactions, improving both the reaction rate and selectivity. The interplay between these effects creates a more favorable reaction environment, enabling reactions that require lower energy input compared to those driven solely by thermal or solar energy.

The interpretation of chemical reactions within the framework of photothermal catalysis depends on understanding the dominant energy source driving the reaction. If the absorbed light primarily generates hot carriers that directly participate in the reaction, the mechanism leans toward photocatalysis. On the other hand, if photon-to-phonon conversion predominates, leading to significant thermal energy generation, the reaction resembles traditional thermocatalysis. Identifying the major energy source for the reaction is critical for designing experiments and interpreting reaction pathways in photothermal catalysis research. Currently, most studies in the field focus on the thermal aspect, analyzing reaction mechanisms through thermocatalysis. This approach is reasonable, given the significant heat generated by plasmonic NPs during light absorption. However, the role of hot carriers cannot be disregarded, as they introduce unique reaction pathways that differ from classical thermocatalysis. The strategic design of materials and catalysts to optimize these effects is critical for advancing the field, particularly in applications such as photothermal CO2 hydrogenation and DRM.

## PHOTOTHERMAL CATALYST FOR CO2 HYDROGENATION

CO2 hydrogenation refers to the reaction between carbon dioxide and hydrogen to produce C1 or C2+ products. While numerous studies have been conducted on producing C2+ compounds, these reactions remain complex and require significant energy input, thus presenting several challenges[[45](#B45)]. Consequently, much of the research has focused on the production of C1 products, which serve as precursors for various industrial applications. The most prominent reactions in this field include CO2 methanation, Reverse Water Gas Shift (RWGS) reaction, and methanol synthesis, which are respectively written as

(1) $$ \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathrm{CO}\_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}\_{2} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{CH}\_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}\_{2} \mathrm{O} \end{aligned} \end{equation} $$

(2) $$ \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathrm{CO}\_{2}+\mathrm{H}\_{2} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}\_{2} \mathrm{O} \end{aligned} \end{equation} $$

(3) $$ \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathrm{CO}\_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}\_{2} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{CH}\_{3} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{H}\_{2} \mathrm{O} \end{aligned} \end{equation} $$

In these processes, CO2 and H2 are used as feedstocks, yielding CH4, CO, and CH3OH, respectively. By utilizing CO2, a major greenhouse gas, these reactions offer significant environmental and industrial benefits by producing essential fuels and chemicals. Both photocatalytic and traditional thermal catalytic methods have been widely explored to facilitate these transformations. However, thermal catalysis requires substantial energy input and often suffers from catalyst deactivation due to coking at high temperatures[[46](#B46)]. Photocatalysis, on the other hand, is limited by its relatively low activity[[47](#B47)]. To address these challenges, there has been a growing emphasis on photothermal catalysis. Photothermal catalytic reactions demonstrate higher efficiency at lower temperatures compared to thermocatalytic processes[[48](#B48)] and gain attention due to their ability to harness abundant solar energy. Both precious metals and conventional transition metals have been utilized in the field of photothermal catalysts. Current research is focused on improving catalytic efficiency by optimizing light absorption and designing economically feasible catalysts. Additionally, many studies are dedicated to verifying the enhancement of photothermal effects through structural modifications of catalytic materials and further elucidating the mechanisms behind these effects.

### Photothermal catalyst for CO2 methanation

CO2 methanation is a process in which CO2 reacts with H2 to produce CH4, as given in:

(4) $$ \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathrm{CO}\_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}\_{2} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{CH}\_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}\_{2} \mathrm{O} \quad\Delta\mathrm{H} \_{R}^{298K} =-165 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} \end{equation} $$

## Recent advances and developments in solar-driven photothermal catalytic CO2 reduction into multicarbon (C2+) products

### Abstract

Solar-driven catalytic conversion of carbon dioxide (CO2) into value-added C2+ chemicals and fuels has attracted significant attention over the past decades, propelled by urgent environmental and energy demands. However, the catalytic reduction of CO2 continues to face significant challenges due to inherently slow reduction kinetics. This review traces the historical development and current state of photothermal CO2 reduction, detailing the mechanisms by which CO2 is transformed into C2+ products. A key focus is on catalyst design, emphasizing surface defect engineering, bifunctional active site and co-catalyst coupling to enhance the efficiency and selectivity of solar-driven C2+ synthesis. Key reaction pathways to both C1 and C2+ products are discussed, ranging from CO, CH4 and methanol (CH3OH) synthesis to the production of C2–4 products such as C2–4 hydrocarbons, ethanol, acetic acid, and various carbonates. Notably, the advanced synthesis of C5+ hydrocarbons exemplifies the remarkable potential of photothermal technologies to effectively upgrade CO2-derived products, thereby delivering sustainable liquid fuels. This review provides a comprehensive overview of fundamental mechanisms, recent breakthroughs, and pathway optimizations, culminating in valuable insights for future research and industrial-scale prospect of photothermal CO2 reduction.

![Graphical abstract: Recent advances and developments in solar-driven photothermal catalytic CO2 reduction into multicarbon (C2+) products](/en/Image/Get?imageInfo.ImageType=GA&imageInfo.ImageIdentifier.ManuscriptID=D5SC00330J&imageInfo.ImageIdentifier.Year=2025)![](https://www.rsc-cdn.org/pubs-core/2022.0.188/content/NewImages/Ajax-GA-Loader.gif)

SUMMARY:
Recently, two main approaches have been devoted to addressing these issues7,8: (1) reducing the utilization of traditional fossil fuels and transitioning to use renewable energy sources (e.g., wind power, solar energy, hydropower)9; (2) implementing carbon capture, geological storage, utilization, and conversion (Figure 2).10,11,12 The catalytic conversion of CO 2 into valuable chemicals such as carbon monoxide, methane, methanol, formic acid, and ethanol through catalytic reactions (e.g., photocatalysis, thermal catalysis, electrocatalysis) has long been recognized as a promising approach to mitigating CO 2 emissions.13,14,15,16,17 Solar-powered conversion of CO 2, often referred to as artificial photosynthesis, provides a promising pathway to reduce CO 2 concentration in atmosphere and holds a potential way mitigate global warming.18 CO 2 reduction products is usually categorized into C1 products (e.g., CO, CH 4, CH 3 OH) and multi-carbon alkanes and olefins (C 2 and C 2+).19,20,21 These C1 small molecules serve as the primary raw materials for manufacturing products such as plastics, fuels, and fertilizers (Figure 2).

Review

Advances in photothermal CO 2 hydrogenation catalysis for C1 molecules
==============================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Yunyun Dong 1, Ruyang Song 1, Zhaorui Zhang 1, Xiao Han 1, Bo Wang 1, Shuo Tao 1, Jinsheng Zhao 1, Abdullah N.Alodhayb 4, Zhou Chen 3, Xiaodong Yi 3, Ningqiang Zhang 2

Open access

Summary
-------

Excessive CO 2 emissions pose challenges in climate and energy sectors. Heterogeneous catalytic CO 2 conversion to valuable chemicals, particularly C1 species, has attracted worldwide attention. However, these processes often necessitate high pressures and temperatures, limiting their practicality. Therefore, it is imperative to develop new CO 2 hydrogenation strategies under milder conditions. Photothermal catalysis offers a promising pathway to reduce energy consumption by combining photocatalysis and thermal catalysis. This review focuses on recent advancements in photothermal CO 2 hydrogenation to C1 products, including reverse water-gas shift, CO 2 methanation, dry reforming of methane, and CO 2 hydrogenation to methanol. In particular, _in situ_ characterization is crucial in elucidating the photothermal catalytic mechanisms. We finally discuss the existing challenges, propose future perspectives, and provide insights into the catalytic mechanism for the selective production of C1 products. This review aims to enhance understanding of photothermal CO 2 conversion and guide future research in solar-driven energy utilization systems.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005502)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005514)

Introduction
------------

Prior to the Industrial Revolution, humans primarily relied on firewood for energy, which is a product of photosynthesis (carbohydrates) generated by plants using CO 2. For an extended period, this simple energy consumption pattern prevented substantial alterations in the net CO 2 content of the atmosphere ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fig1)).[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib2) However, since the Industrial Revolution from the 1860s, fossil fuels (coal, oil etc.) have been extensively utilized for power generation, heating, and transportation, resulting in an exponential rise in atmospheric CO 2 concentrations.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib4) The substantial emissions of CO 2 from human activities have contributed to abnormal global climate patterns, intensified ocean storms, and increased desertification. These factors have culminated in a series of catastrophic consequences, including rising sea levels and rapid alterations to the Earth’s current ecosystem, posing significant threats to human survival and development.[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib5) In 2018, the United Nations Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) pointed out that it is very urgent to reduce global temperature at least 1.5°C. This climate-change control target is widely recognized not only by academia and the United Nations but also by governments worldwide.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib6) Achieving this goal necessitates the attainment of net-zero carbon emissions. Consequently, mitigating CO 2 emissions and promoting its rational utilization have emerged as the central guiding principles for addressing environmental challenges.

Figure 1. Conceptual figure exemplifying the carbon cycle

The figure illustrates major elements of the carbon cycle, which was significantly changed by increased fossil fuel burning.

Recently, two main approaches have been devoted to addressing these issues[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib8): (1) reducing the utilization of traditional fossil fuels and transitioning to use renewable energy sources (e.g., wind power, solar energy, hydropower)[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib9); (2) implementing carbon capture, geological storage, utilization, and conversion ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fig2)).[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib12) The catalytic conversion of CO 2 into valuable chemicals such as carbon monoxide, methane, methanol, formic acid, and ethanol through catalytic reactions (e.g., photocatalysis, thermal catalysis, electrocatalysis) has long been recognized as a promising approach to mitigating CO 2 emissions.[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib13),[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib14),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib16),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib17) Solar-powered conversion of CO 2, often referred to as artificial photosynthesis, provides a promising pathway to reduce CO 2 concentration in atmosphere and holds a potential way mitigate global warming.[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib18) CO 2 reduction products is usually categorized into C1 products (e.g., CO, CH 4, CH 3 OH) and multi-carbon alkanes and olefins (C 2 and C 2+).[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib19),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib20),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib21) These C1 small molecules serve as the primary raw materials for manufacturing products such as plastics, fuels, and fertilizers ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fig2)).

Figure 2. Conceptual figure exemplifying the major steps of reducing CO 2 emissions and increasing rational utilization

An example of where thermocatalysis may fit into the carbon-reuse process is shown.

Photocatalysis, a technology proposed by G. Ciamician in 1912, harnesses light energy to provide excitation energy for chemical reactions.[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib22) Despite its long history, it did not garner significant attention until 1972, when Fujishima et al. discovered that titanium dioxide can catalyze the splitting of water into hydrogen and oxygen.[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib23) In recent years, many review articles and perspectives have been proposed on photocatalytic CO 2 reduction, providing a comprehensive overview of the current state of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction, encompassing recent advancements, catalysts design strategies, challenges, solving strategies, and more.[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib19),[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib24) The reduction process, however, involves several intricate steps, such as the challenges of adsorption/activation of CO 2 molecules, charge recombination, limited light absorption, poor photostability of the catalyst materials, as well as the unidentified catalytic mechanism limited the development of photocatalytic performance of target C1 products.

Photothermal catalysis with advantages of photocatalysis and thermocatalysis by introducing heat energy was proposed to enhance sunlight utilization, promote photogenerated charge carrier excitation and separation, accelerate the diffusion of reactive molecules, and improve the overall catalytic conversion performance.[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib25),[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib26) As shown in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#tbl1), we summarize the main characteristics of thermal, photocatalytic, and photothermal CO 2 conversion technologies, highlighting the differences in reaction routes, products, and advantages and disadvantages of each technology. Compared to traditional photochemical CO 2 catalysis, photothermal catalysis has potential to harnesses the full solar spectrum, which could improve efficient conversion of CO 2 to C1 chemicals under mild conditions.[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib27)[Scheme 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#sch1) provides a concise overview of CO 2 reduction via photothermal catalysis from 2016 to 2023, encompassing the catalytic conversion of C1 molecules (methane, carbon monoxide, and methanol).[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib28),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib29),[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib30),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib31),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib32),[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib33),[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib34),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib35),[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib36),[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib37),[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib38),[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib39),[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib40),[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib41),[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib42),[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib43),[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib44),[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib45),[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib46) Despite the progress made, the system still faces challenges, including low conversion rates, poor product selectivity, and inefficient light energy utilization. Concurrently, a comprehensive understanding of the photothermal effect and its synergistic effects is crucial for comprehending and enhancing the performance of photothermal catalysis. Prior reviews have extensively covered the synthesis and characterization of photothermochemical materials.[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib47),[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib48) However, the intricate catalytic pathways involved in CO 2 conversion to C1 molecules is inadequate, and the exploration of advanced instrumental techniques for _in situ_ or _operando_ mechanistic studies of photothermal catalysis is lacking.

| CO 2 hydrogenation technologies | Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Thermocatalysis | * •high conversion rate * •easy for industrialization * •tandem routes for value-added chemicals | * •high energy consumption and safety cost * •inferior selectivity and poor stability * •coke formation |
| Photocatalysis | * •environmental benign * •sunlight sensitivity | * •limited light utilization * •low conversion efficiency * •poor stability |

Scheme 1. A brief overview of reduction of CO 2 by photothermal catalytic reaction from 2016 to 2023

In this comprehensive review, we summarize an updated discussion of recent developments in photothermal CO 2 conversion. We begin by providing a concise overview of the mechanism of photothermal CO 2 conversion, highlighting the primary driving force. Subsequently, based on the varying reaction products, we offer a detailed introduction to the catalyst selection, reaction conditions, and reaction mechanism of thermally assisted photocatalytic reactions. We underscore the importance of monitoring key intermediates formed during catalytic reactions and the dynamic evolution of catalyst microstructure in real time using advanced _in situ_ characterization techniques. These techniques offer insights into the potential mechanisms of photothermal catalytic CO 2 conversion. Furthermore, we categorize the current concepts and principles of photothermal catalytic CO 2 reduction and provide a systematic overview of the research progress in thermal-assisted photocatalytic CO 2 reduction. To address the need for milder CO 2 hydrogenation conditions, promising strategies have been developed to convert CO 2 into valuable chemical products. Among these, sunlight-powered photothermal processes that combine thermal and light energy have gained significant attention. Finally, we discuss the development of photo-assisted thermal-catalytic CO 2 reduction for C1 chemical technology. Future research is encouraged to focus on enhancing CO 2 conversion rate and product selectivity, while simultaneously exploring the reaction mechanism using advanced _in situ_ characterization techniques is crucial to further develop the photothermal catalytic CO 2 conversion.

Mechanism of photothermal catalysis
-----------------------------------

CO 2 is a non-polar, linear molecule featuring two identical three-center, four-electron delocalized π bonds. These bonds have very high thermodynamic stability and are chemically inert due to their characteristics similar to triple bonds, which greatly limits the activation of CO 2 molecules (the dissociation energy of C=O bond is about 750 kJ mol−1).[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib49) Different from the above single thermal catalysis or photocatalysis, photothermal catalysis is a highly efficient catalytic technology that integrates photochemical and thermochemical processes.[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib50),[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib51) Based on the interaction patterns between photon and thermal energy, the photothermal catalysis pathways have currently been classified to photo-assisted thermocatalysis, photo-driven thermocatalysis, thermal-assisted photocatalysis, and photothermal co-catalysis ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fig3)).[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib19),[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib52),[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib53),[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib54)

Figure 3. The four interaction patterns between photon (solar picture) and thermal (fire picture) energy

(A) Photo-assisted thermocatalysis in which heat energy is the primary driving force, while solar plays a supporting role.

(B) Photo-driven thermocatalysis in which the thermal effect is introduced by solar, without any independent photocatalytic activity.

(C) Thermal-assisted photocatalysis in which light energy is the primary driving force, while heat assists the reaction.

(D) Photothermal co-catalysis in which solar energy and heat energy act synergistically to drive the reaction. M, metal oxide active site on catalyst surface.

Photo-assisted thermocatalysis
------------------------------

In photo-assisted thermocatalysis ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fig3)A), heat is a primary driving force, while light serves as an auxiliary factor that enhances the thermocatalytic process, rather than driving photocatalysis. Catalysts in this mode exhibit predominantly thermocatalytic activity, utilizing sunlight to facilitate the rate-determining step of thermocatalysis. This process can enhance the catalysts’ surface local temperature via electron relaxation and non-radiative recombination, which effectively lowers the apparent activation barrier for thermocatalysis. In current research, thermocouple placement within the sample bed is commonly employed for monitoring catalyst temperature. Infrared (IR) imaging, however, offers more accurate measurements of the catalyst surface temperature.[55](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib55),[56](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib56) Additionally, scanning thermal microscope (SThM) enables the acquisition of nanoscale-resolution local temperature maps by analyzing the temperature-dependent energy transfer of plasma peaks.[57](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib57) This technique boasts a spatial resolution of 10 nm and an accuracy ranging from ∼10 to 50 mK. The microscopic local temperature (_T_ local) of the catalyst can be estimated from the composition of different gases when the photothermal CO 2 hydrogenation reaction reaches equilibrium.[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib38)

Photothermochemical cycles can be initiated at lower temperatures, unlike thermochemical cycles, which necessitate extremely high temperatures. For example, this synergistic effect was found in CO 2-to-CO conversion ([Figure 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fig4)A).[58](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib58) Zhang et al. proposed a two-step strategy for the photothermochemical cycle.[59](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#bib59) Photons activate the catalyst under UV or sunlight irradiation, generating oxygen vacancies (Ovs) ([Equation 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fd1)). In the latter reaction process, CO 2 was catalyzed by Ovs to form CO with a thermal catalysis process ([Equation 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666386424005204#fd2)).(Equation 1)M x O y→M x O y−1+1/2 O 2(g)(photocatalytic reaction,room temperature)(Equation 2)M x O y−1+CO 2→M x O y+CO(g)(thermocatalytic reaction,>573 K)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements in photothermal catalysts enhance CO₂ reduction efficiency by utilizing light and heat synergistically, enabling lower activation energy and higher conversion rates.
Innovative electrochemical techniques for CO₂ reduction have been developed, optimizing electrode materials and operational parameters to improve selectivity and yield of desired products.
Integrating renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind, into CO₂ reduction processes supports sustainability and reduces reliance on fossil fuels, promoting a circular carbon economy.

Evaluating sustainability metrics, including energy efficiency, carbon footprint reduction, and resource utilization, is crucial to assess the environmental impact of photothermal CO₂ reduction technologies.
